item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm on the fence with this one. On the one hand, it's really interesting and the historic reason makes sense, on the other, it's never really explained how this thing could "hurt" the Foundation, so it ends up sounding like they want to appease it just because. That doesn't sound right to me. I'll downvote, but let me know if you ever explain that part and I'll gladly upvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so that blackboxed skip in the description suggests that the Foudnation recovered another item from inside SCP-2807, likely an anomalous object that was stored there after being recovered from the local area. SCP-1215 is just such an object. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2807 is located in Eastern Turkey, near the ancient ruined city of Ani.
SCP-2807 refers to Site-04 in its entirety except for the control center, which exists separately from the rest of site. SCP-2807 refers to a previously abandoned Foundation storage facility, estimated to be more than 200 years old, and most likely abandoned after the [REDACTED] event. Despite several breach attempts, the storage facilities inside remained intact. SCP-████ and several archives were recovered.

To this:

SCP-2807 is located in Eastern Turkey, near the ancient ruined city of Ani.
SCP-2807 refers to Site-04 in its entirety except for the control center, which exists separately from the rest of site. SCP-2807 refers to a previously abandoned Foundation storage facility, estimated to be more than 200 years old, and most likely abandoned after the [REDACTED] event. Despite several breach attempts, the storage facilities inside remained intact. SCP-1215 and several archives were recovered.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but I'm not certain of the execution. I think part of it is that it confused me, initially, and that my mind for some reason isn't parsing everything quite correctly right now.

That said, I think I'm going to leave this up as a tab and re-read it later and vote then. Right now, though, I'm leaning towards an upvote.

E: Got stuck in a very dull class, so I ended up re-reading this much earlier than planned. Turns out it just had to sit for a while. Nice job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

This one’s a pretty interesting. It reminds me of Hallway 4, D-Class Dorms, Site 14, which is a good thing and a bad thing.

A good thing in that the psychic/spiritual scars of past D-Class hell infuses and congealed into a force that persists even worse than the events that bore it.

A less good thing in that I feel the D-Class Hallway is a little more subtle, this one is alright in its own way.

This one’s also a lot more… robust, in-universe, than the 479. It’s evident something exceptionally wrong happened here. Exactly what is unclear even to the Foundation.

I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's actually an error from a previous edit, it is now rectified.

Also, not sure why everyone assumes ghost - when I visualized it, it was a malevolent force, a composite of all the D-class that suffered there - but it's open to interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Novoting because it just didn't grab me enough. 'Malicious D-class ghost' feels a little boring to me, even if it is hidden behind a decent format screw.
Also shouldn't the Keter designation be in blue because it's changed in the collapsible? And the original description describes the facility as 'more than 200 years old', while the collapsible version has 300 years. Is that an error or should the '300' be blue as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote. I had expected to downvote when I saw that this was a D-Class specific anomaly, which I was kind of afraid I'd see done repeatedly during the contest, and then when I saw the fake-out document. However, the collapsibles told a decent enough story, to the point that I was interested in finding out what happened. I'm also a huge sucker for ghost stories. I was sufficiently entertained to the point that I elected to not downvote.

Also, kind of funny that this is the second piece in the contest that I've read that connects the Foundation to the Confederate states.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for clearing that up! I had actually assumed it said it was out of operation that long. But upon rereading I see that the time it shut down isn't stated. Either way it doesn't impact the article as a whole negatively for me, it's just something that took me out of it a little bit. It's probably just personal preference so if you like it the way it is, I say don't change them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, they're not necessarily intended to be that old. The site itself is at least 200 years old, but not necessarily 200 years out of operation. That's intentionally left ambiguous. I also think that we often overestimate how 'old-sounding' things are, but concede the point that some of them are probably a little too modern.

Otherwise, I'm glad you liked the concept and format screw. And yes, you've nailed the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not crazy about the tone of the notes in the 2nd drop down. They read like they were written in a very modern tone of voice for things meant to be written by people from a couple hundred years ago (with the exception of "I've been here six and thirty weeks."). Aside from that my only nitpick is the phrase "after the [REDACTED] event." feels clunky to me. I'd rather it simply read "after [REDACTED]", but that's probably just my personal preference. Nothing worth a downvote as far as I'm concerned.

Onto what I loved! The use of the blue text to show the parts that had been removed from the "incomplete" entry was fantastic. I was impressed by how both the complete and incomplete versions read very well. If not for the reveal in the drop down, it wasn't obvious to me that anything had been removed.

As for the idea, if I understand it correctly, I love it. The Foundation mentally breaking people to have a kind of "blank slate" human test subject, is just dripping with creepyness. I also like the tie-in to the creation of amnestics being the reason the project was no longer necessary and shut down.

You get a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is incredible! So many good pieces came out of this contest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't mean that this particular premise was done before. I just felt like I've read something similar in the past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you find it, could you link it? I try to avoid duplicating ideas, but maybe something slipped through the cracks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The wrath of the people who knew too much-turned-D Class made into an anomaly? Pretty sure this has been done before but I'll go with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was pretty wary of this until you got to the excerpts at the bottom of the article, then a lot of the pieces fell into place, and the article as whole snapped together.

I do like the progress that is visible in terms of the path the Foundation took in originally creating D-class personnel, and that multiple sites were dedicated for that exclusive purpose. It paints a suitably dark and creepy picture of the Foundation.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title had me kind of eeeeeh. But you made it work wonderfully. Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's my D-class SCP. Thanks to everyone in Site 19 and the forum who gave crit to make this as good as it is.
